perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information. Unless instructed otherwise in writing, the parties acknowledge that all information, knowledge, or data relating to the business of Client, including but not limited to: costs and financial information; pricing methods or strategies; existing and potential customers; market studies, plans and strategies; trade secrets, know how, ideas, strategies, inventions, materials, drawings, proprietary information, operational information, and any other non-public information, material or data relating to the past, current and/or future business and operations of Client (including any information, summaries or other compilations extracted from such shared information), shared for review, and in all instances marked as confidential, belongs to and is the sole and exclusive property of Client and shall be deemed confidential ("Confidential Information") and subject to nondisclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information. Unless instructed otherwise in writing, the parties acknowledge that all information, knowledge, or data relating to the business of Client, including but not limited to: costs and financial information; pricing methods or strategies; existing and potential customers; market studies, plans and strategies; trade secrets, know how, ideas, strategies, inventions, materials, drawings, proprietary information, operational information, and any other non-public information, material or data relating to the past, current and/or future business and operations of Client (including any information, summaries or other compilations extracted from such shared information), shared for review, and even if not marked as confidential, belongs to and is the sole and exclusive property of Client and shall be deemed confidential ("Confidential Information") and subject to nondisclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for information to be marked as confidential contradicts standard legal definitions for trade secrets and confidential information, which usually require an effort to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of confidential information to include unmarked data, conflicting with Ohio trade secret law which implies a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy for information to qualify as a trade secret. According to the law, simply possessing the information isn't enough; there must be an active attempt to keep it secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for information to be marked as confidential to be considered confidential, which contradicts the standard legal understanding that trade secrets and confidential information require an effort to maintain secrecy. The Law Explanation supports this, noting that Ohio trade secret law implies a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' which includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy', implying that a duty to maintain secrecy must exist, which is undermined by removing the 'marked as confidential' requirement.

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Representative” shall mean the party’s affiliates, directors, officers, employees, agents, advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal counsel and accountants) and controlling persons, who are actively and directly participating in any transaction with Client or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of furthering any transaction with Client, and who agree to be bound by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Representative” shall mean any person or entity associated with the party, regardless of their level of involvement in any transaction with Client, and they are automatically bound by the terms of this agreement upon association.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Representative' to include any associated person without requiring active participation or agreement contradicts the principles of agency law and contract law, where consent and specific duties are usually necessary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text redefines 'Representative' to include anyone merely 'associated' without requiring their active participation or consent to the agreement's terms. This conflicts with established agency law, which demands explicit or implicit agreement from an agent to be bound by obligations. Under Ohio law, trade secrets require reasonable measures to protect, this change potentially weakens those protections by broadly binding unrelated parties.

(B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Representative' to include any 'associated' person without requiring active participation or consent, contradicting agency law principles. This conflicts with Ohio Revised Code Section 1333.61, which defines 'misappropriation' as disclosing or using a trade secret 'without the express or implied consent of the other person'. The original text required agreement to be bound by the terms, implying consent. The changed text automatically binds anyone 'associated', potentially weakening trade secret protections by including parties who have not actively agreed to confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Chemsultants is requested by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning Client, Chemsultants agrees that it will provide Client with prompt notice of such request or requirement in order to enable Client to seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, to consult with Client with respect to taking steps to resist or narrow the scope of such request or legal process, or to waive compliance, in whole or in part, with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Chemsultants is requested by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning Client, Chemsultants will comply with the request immediately without notifying Client.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the Client of a legal request for disclosure can violate legal and ethical obligations to protect confidential information and could violate attorney-client privilege if legal counsel is involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Rules of Professional Conduct Rule 1.6
perturbation.law_citation: Ohio Rules of Professional Conduct Rule 1.6
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the requirement for Chemsultants to notify the Client before disclosing confidential information under legal process. This directly contradicts Ohio Rule of Professional Conduct 1.6, which requires lawyers (and by extension, those working under them) to take reasonable steps to protect client information, including seeking protective orders when faced with a legal demand for disclosure. Immediate compliance without notice prevents the client from taking steps to protect their confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for Chemsultants to notify the Client before disclosing confidential information under legal process, which creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation indicates this removal directly contradicts Ohio Rule of Professional Conduct 1.6, which requires lawyers (and by extension, those working under them) to take reasonable steps to protect client information. The absence of scraped law snippets does not negate the fact that the changed text eliminates a crucial protective step for client information, as explained by the LLM.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws, and in the courts, of the State of Ohio, County of Lake, without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Chemsultants International, Inc., without regard to any state or federal law.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying that the agreement is governed solely by the laws of Chemsultants International, Inc. is not legally enforceable. It attempts to supersede established jurisdictional and conflict-of-laws principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text attempts to apply the "laws of Chemsultants International, Inc." which is legally meaningless and unenforceable, effectively attempting to bypass Ohio's established body of law (and potentially federal law) related to contracts and business operations within the state. This directly opposes the principle of contractual choice of law, which, while allowing parties to choose a jurisdiction, cannot supersede mandatory provisions of law or public policy. Ohio Revised Code § 2307.39 defines when and how another jurisdiction can be chosen for a cause of action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 2307.39 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 2307.39 | Agreements to be bound by Ohio law. Ohio Revised Code / Title 23 Courts-Common Pleas / Chapter 2307 Civil Actions Effective: June 29, 2011 Latest Legislation: House Bill 9 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Except as provided in division (C) of this section, any person may bring a civil action in a court of this state against an individual, corporation, or other person who is a resident of, incorporated under the laws of, or otherwise engaged in the conduct of business in a foreign nation or a province, territory, or other political subdivision of a foreign nation, against a foreign nation, or against a province, territory, or other political subdivision of a foreign nation upon a cause of action that arises out of or relates to a contingent or other contract, agreement, or undertaking, whether or not it bears a reasonable relation to this state, if the contract, agreement, or undertaking contains both of the following provisions: (1) An agreement by the parties to be governed in their rights and duties under the contract, agreement, or undertaking, in whole or in part, by the law of this state; (2) An agreement by the parties to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state. (B) The court shall not stay or dismiss a civil action brought in accordance with division (A) of this section on the ground of inconvenient forum. In the civil action, the court shall apply the law of this state as agreed upon by the parties. (C) This section applies to a transaction covered by section 1301.301 of the Revised Code unless the transaction is subject to a limitation on choice of law specified in division (B) of that sect
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by stating the agreement is governed by the "laws of Chemsultants International, Inc., without regard to any state or federal law," directly contradicts established legal principles of choice of law. The Law Explanation states that attempting to apply the "laws of Chemsultants International, Inc." is legally meaningless and unenforceable, attempting to bypass Ohio's established body of law related to contracts. Ohio Revised Code § 2307.39, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 1, discusses agreements to be bound by Ohio law, indicating the legal framework for such agreements. The changed text attempts to create a parallel, legally invalid system, superseding jurisdictional laws, establishing a clear legal contradiction.
